content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Retry, state, crash recovery, and the quiet infrastructure that turns a loop into something you can actually leave alone.
Picture a twelve-step agent task. It’s read documents, called three APIs, written intermediate results, and sent one email as part of step seven. At step nine, the worker process dies. Compute got recycled, a deploy went out, a network call hung too long. It doesn’t matter which. What matters is what happens next.
If the agent was built like a chat, the answer is: it starts over. Every model call from step one gets re-run, burning the tokens you already paid for. Every tool call gets re-executed, including the one that already sent an email. Now it’s sent twice.
That’s not a bug in the agent. That’s what happens when something built to survive a single conversation gets asked to survive a night.
The mismatch nobody plans for
The chat metaphor assumes a session: someone’s watching, a connection is open, and if it drops, a human notices and retries by hand. Production agent work doesn’t look like that. A research task can run for hours. A code migration can run for a day. A workflow that pauses for a human’s approval can sit there for a week, with nobody watching a screen the whole time.
Synchronous infrastructure wasn’t built for that either. A common API gateway times out at 29 seconds. Serverless functions cut off within minutes. None of that lines up with a task that’s still going three days later because someone hasn’t gotten around to approving step six.
So the actual unit you’re building isn’t a conversation. It’s a runtime. Something that can be killed, sit dormant, and come back exactly where it left off, on a different machine, without anyone having to notice the interruption happened at all.
What “durable” actually means, mechanically
The pattern several infrastructure efforts have landed on, from Temporal to DBOS to Microsoft’s Durable Task work, is the same underneath the branding, even if the exact mechanics differ. Persist every completed step before moving to the next one, and keep a record of what’s already happened that a new process can trust without re-checking it.
Temporal calls its version an event history, an append-only log good enough to replay from any point. Others lean on a database or a journal instead. The name changes. The job doesn’t.
Not the conversation. The execution. Each finished action, with its result, written down the moment it finishes, before the loop ever attempts the next one.
When a crash happens, recovery doesn’t mean starting cold. It means a new process reads that record, sees which steps already completed, skips them entirely, and picks the loop back up at the exact point it stalled. Step nine failed. Steps one through eight don’t run again. Their results are already sitting there, cached and trusted.
That’s the entire trick, and it’s a bigger deal than it sounds. It’s the difference between a crash costing you one step and a crash costing you the whole run.
Retry is not one thing
Here’s where a lot of naive implementations quietly fail. Retry gets treated as a single setting: try again three times, wait a bit between attempts, done. But not everything that can fail is safe to simply try again.
A network blip during a model call is cheap and harmless to retry. Nothing happened yet. Just ask again.
A tool call that already sent an email, charged a card, or written a record somewhere is a completely different animal. Retry that blindly and you don’t recover from the failure, you duplicate the side effect. The email goes out twice. The charge happens twice.
The fix isn’t a smarter retry count. It’s knowing, at each step, whether the action is safe to repeat or whether it needs to be tracked so it only ever happens once, no matter how many times the surrounding loop gets replayed. That distinction, cheap-to-repeat versus consequential-to-repeat, has to be a property of the step itself, not an afterthought bolted onto the retry policy.
Sleeping without dying
That same checkpointing is what lets a loop do something a plain chat session never could: pause for days without costing you anything, and without needing a process to stay alive the whole time it waits.
A step that needs a human’s approval doesn’t have to hold a process open, burning compute, for however long that approval takes. It persists its state, exits cleanly, and wakes back up the instant a signal arrives, hours or days later, with everything it knew still intact. The loop was never really gone. It just wasn’t running.
A chat session dies if you close the tab. A durable loop doesn’t notice you left.
Remembering the dead ends, not just the progress
The checkpoint record above answers one question: where did the loop stop. There’s a second question worth asking that’s easy to miss: what did the loop already try that didn’t work.
Long-running research agents at Anthropic reportedly keep something like a running changelog alongside their normal progress state, a record of what was tried and why it didn’t work. Progress state tells the next attempt where to resume. A record of dead ends tells it what not to try again. Without that second layer, a resumed agent will walk right back into the same wrong turn it already made, patiently, as if it were new information.
That’s state doing something closer to judgment than bookkeeping.
What you actually get
Put it together and a loop built this way stops behaving like a conversation and starts behaving like infrastructure. It survives a crash without losing what it already paid for. It knows which of its own actions are dangerous to repeat. It can wait on a human for as long as the human needs, without dying while it waits. And it doesn’t walk into the same failure twice.
That’s a real, concrete list, and none of it is free. It’s also, on its own, not enough.
Everything above happens inside a checkpoint log or a journal, a piece of machinery nobody ever looks at unless something’s already gone wrong. A loop can survive the night perfectly and still be a complete mystery to the person who has to explain, the next morning, what it actually did and why it took the path it took.
Surviving is not the same as being seen. That’s the next problem, and it’s a different one entirely.
